SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper presents clear empirical claims (brute-force pruning outperforms first/second-order Taylor approximations; large fractions of neurons can be removed without retraining) and provides experiments on toy tasks and MNIST subsets supporting those claims, but several technical weaknesses limit confidence. Key mathematical derivations are outsourced to an appendix (not provided) so correctness and details of the second-order/Hessian computations cannot be verified; the authors also rely on the strong diagonal-Hessian and serial-pruning assumptions (acknowledged) without quantifying their impact. The experimental methodology lacks important controls: reduced/altered MNIST subset, no reporting of multiple runs, variances, statistical tests, or implementation details (random seeds, training stability), so results may not be robust. Overall the empirical observations are plausible and partially supported, but missing proofs/details and limited experimental rigor justify a middle score.

CLARITY.justification: The paper has a clear overall structure (Introduction → Related Work → Methods → Experiments → Conclusion) and states the main contribution up front (a neuron-level pruning algorithm without re‑training). However, presentation suffers from repeated/garbled blocks of text and inconsistent figure references (e.g. “Figure ??”, out‑of‑order mentions like Figure 21) that impede reading. Notation is mostly consistent (Ok, ∂E/∂O, Taylor expansions) but some second‑order terms and assumptions are deferred to the appendix and not fully motivated in the main text. Figures and visualizations are described helpfully, but the missing/poorly referenced figures and occasional grammatical/repetition errors reduce clarity.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper addresses a well-studied problem (network pruning) and explicitly builds on prior work (e.g. Skeletonization, Optimal Brain Damage/Surgeon) — the authors themselves state they “borrow much of our inspiration” and note Skeletonization already targets units (Section 2.2). Its main technical novelty is applying/deriving a second-order Taylor approximation at the whole-neuron level and an iterative re-ranking pruning routine (Sections 3.2–3.3), plus systematic brute-force comparisons and error-surface visualizations. Those are useful and non-trivial empirical/algorithmic extensions, but they are incremental relative to existing second-order and unit-pruning literature rather than a fundamentally new paradigm.
